---
title: "Week 2: Simple Linear Regression I"
date: "2026-08-24"
---

## Overview

This week introduces the simple linear regression (SLR) model, the building block for everything that follows in the course. You will learn how ordinary least squares (OLS) estimation finds the best-fitting line through a cloud of data points, how to interpret the slope and intercept in context, and how R-squared summarizes model fit. These ideas carry directly into every subsequent regression topic.

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this week, you will be able to:

- Write the population and sample SLR equations and define each term
- Explain how OLS minimizes the sum of squared residuals to estimate slope and intercept
- Interpret the slope and intercept in the context of a research problem
- Calculate and interpret R-squared as the proportion of variance explained
- Create and interpret scatterplots with fitted regression lines in R

## Readings

- {{< fa book-open >}} **Required**: [Chapter 1: Simple Linear Regression]({{< var books.lecture_notes >}}/chapters/01-simple-linear-regression.html){target="_blank"}

## R Lab

- {{< fa laptop-code >}} [Lab 02: Simple Regression in R]({{< var books.r_lab_book >}}/labs/lab02-simple-regression.html){target="_blank"}

## Key Concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **SLR model** | A model predicting an outcome from a single predictor using a straight line |
| **OLS estimation** | A method that finds slope and intercept by minimizing the sum of squared residuals |
| **Slope (b1)** | The expected change in Y for a one-unit increase in X |
| **Intercept (b0)** | The predicted value of Y when X equals zero |
| **Residual** | The difference between an observed Y value and its predicted value |
| **R-squared** | The proportion of variance in Y explained by the linear relationship with X |
| **Fitted value** | The predicted Y for a given X based on the estimated regression equation |
